text.title: Data Analysis Framework
text.summary: Data analysis components, five-step process, three modeling levels, and entity-relationship notation.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Identify data needs: for each user role and system function, ask what information is displayed, captured, calculated, stored, and shared.
rule.rationale: Skipping need identification produces a data model that stores what developers guess rather than what the business requires.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Define data elements: document each attribute with name, business definition, type, format, valid values, source, and owner.
rule.rationale: Without business definitions, developers implement technical interpretations that conflict across systems and reports.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Create data model at three levels: conceptual (high-level entities and relationships for business stakeholders), logical (detailed attributes and rules for BA and developers), physical (tables, columns, indexes for database developers).
rule.rationale: Mixing levels produces models that are too abstract for developers or too technical for business review.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Analyze data quality across six dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness. Measure each with a quantitative metric before defining improvement targets.
rule.rationale: Unmeasured data quality produces subjective "bad data" complaints with no baseline to track improvement against.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Define data rules: document validation rules, derivation formulas, default values, constraints, and uniqueness requirements as testable statements.
rule.rationale: Business rules embedded in code without documentation become invisible and are lost when developers change; they must live in the data dictionary.

rule.statement: Measure accuracy as error rate (% of records with incorrect values vs. authoritative source), completeness as missing value %, consistency as conflict count across systems, timeliness as average data age, validity as % of records violating business rules, uniqueness as duplicate rate.
rule.rationale: Each dimension requires a distinct metric; a single "data quality score" hides which dimension is failing and which remediation to apply.

section.title: Modeling Levels
section.p: Conceptual: entities and relationships only, no attributes. Audience: business stakeholders. Purpose: confirm scope.
Logical: entities with all attributes, data types, relationships with cardinality, business rules. Audience: BA, developers. Purpose: design contract.
Physical: tables, columns, indexes, foreign keys, partitioning. Audience: database developers. Purpose: implementation spec.
